mappings.rationaleAI: Section A.1 covers Pythagoreanism (A.1.a), the Sophists (A.1.b), Eleaticism (A.1.c), and Atomism (A.1.d).
Section A.1.e covers Platonism as a major philosophical school, and B.1 discusses it as a metaphysical theory.
Section A.1.f covers Aristotelianism as a philosophical school, and B.1 discusses it as a metaphysical theory.
Section A.1.g covers Stoicism as a major philosophical school in antiquity.
Section A.1.h covers Epicureanism as a major philosophical school in antiquity.
Section A.1.i covers Skepticism as a philosophical school, and C.2 discusses it as a theory of knowledge validity.
Section A.1.j covers Scholasticism, and B.1 lists Thomism as a type of metaphysical theory.
Section A.1.j covers Scholasticism as a philosophical school, encompassing major medieval thinkers.
Section A.2.a covers Cartesianism as a philosophical school, and B.1 lists it as a metaphysical theory.
Section A.2.b covers Empiricism, to which Locke is a foundational contributor.
Hume is central to Empiricism (A.2.b) and Skepticism, both major philosophical schools.
Section A.2.c covers Rationalism, to which Spinoza is a central contributor.
Section A.2.c covers Rationalism, to which Leibniz is a central contributor.
Section A.2.e covers Kantianism as a major philosophical school.
Section A.2.g covers Hegelianism, and A.2.f covers Idealism to which Hegel is central.
Section A.2.j covers Marxism, and B.1 lists dialectical Materialism as a metaphysical theory.
Section A.2.h covers Utilitarianism as a major philosophical school.
Mill is central to Utilitarianism (A.2.h) and Empiricism (A.2.b).
Section A.2.n covers Existentialism, and B.3 discusses it as a conception of man.
Section A.2.o covers Analytic and Linguistic philosophy as a major modern school.
Encompasses Phenomenology (A.2.m), Existentialism (A.2.n), and Marxism (A.2.j).
Spans Rationalism (A.2.c), Kantianism (A.2.e), Idealism (A.2.f), Hegelianism (A.2.g), and Phenomenology (A.2.m).
Encompasses Cartesianism (A.2.a), Existentialism (A.2.n), Positivism (A.2.i), and Phenomenology (A.2.m).
Hobbes is relevant to Materialism (A.2.d) and Empiricism (A.2.b).
Kierkegaard is foundational for Existentialism (A.2.n).
Nietzsche challenges multiple philosophical schools and relates to theories of conduct (D) and conceptions of man (B.3).
Schopenhauer relates to Idealism (A.2.f) and the reaction against Rationalism.
Heidegger relates to Phenomenology (A.2.m) and Existentialism (A.2.n).
Wittgenstein is central to Analytic and Linguistic philosophy (A.2.o) and Positivism/Logical Empiricism (A.2.i).
Russell is foundational in Analytic philosophy (A.2.o) and contributed to Realism (A.2.k) and Logical Empiricism (A.2.i).
Foucault engages with Marxism (A.2.j) and Phenomenology (A.2.m).
Derrida's deconstruction engages with and challenges Phenomenology (A.2.m) and other schools.
Habermas engages with Marxism (A.2.j), Pragmatism (A.2.l), and Positivism (A.2.i).
Adorno's critical theory engages with Marxism (A.2.j), Idealism (A.2.f), and Positivism (A.2.i).
Critical theory emerged from Marxism (A.2.j) and engages with multiple philosophical schools.
Postmodernism challenges Realism (A.2.k), Positivism (A.2.i), and other traditional positions.
Poststructuralism extends beyond Phenomenology (A.2.m) and Linguistic philosophy (A.2.o).
Section B.2 lists humanism as a view concerning God, and B.3 lists it as a conception of man.
Section B.2 covers atheism as a view concerning the existence of God.
Section B.2 covers agnosticism as a view concerning the existence and knowledge of God.
Section B.2 covers different views on God including theism, Deism, fideism, and pantheism.
Section B.2 covers theism, Deism, and other views concerning God's existence and attributes.
Section C covers theories of thought and knowledge including different conceptions of objects, validity, and sources.
Section C.1 covers conceptions of the object of knowledge including Phenomenalism, Idealism, and Realism.
Section B.1 covers different types of metaphysical theory from Platonism through Organismic dynamism.
Section B.4 covers views on mind and body including Materialism, dualism, and immaterialism.
Free will connects to theories of conduct (D) and the mind-body problem (B.4).
Objectivity relates to Realism (C.1), Kantianism (C.2), and Positivism (C.2).
Arendt engages with Existentialism (A.2.n), Pragmatism (A.2.l), and political philosophy doctrines.
Weil engages with Platonism (A.1.e), mysticism, and ethical doctrines (D).
Augustine is relevant to Platonism (A.1.e) and Scholasticism (A.1.j).
Rousseau relates to theories of conduct (D) and the Enlightenment critique of Rationalism.
Indian philosophy provides alternative metaphysical and epistemological frameworks relevant to B and C.
Avicenna contributed to Scholasticism (A.1.j) and engaged with Aristotelianism (A.1.f) in the Islamic tradition.
Maimonides contributed to Scholasticism (A.1.j) through his rationalist methodology within Jewish philosophy.
Islamic philosophy engaged with Aristotelianism (A.1.f) and Neoplatonism, contributing to Scholasticism (A.1.j).
Covers the ancient schools listed in A.1 including Platonism, Aristotelianism, Stoicism, Epicureanism, and Skepticism.
